solution: Repeated division (the Euclidean algorithm) reduces the pair until the remainder is 0, giving \( \gcd([[a]],[[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the least common multiple, not the greatest common divisor.
This is the product of the two numbers, not their gcd.
This is just the difference of the two numbers, which is not generally the gcd.
This assumes the two numbers are always coprime, which need not be true.
This is one more than the correct gcd.
This is one less than the correct gcd.

stem: What is the least common multiple of \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \)?
solution: Since \( \mathrm{lcm}(a,b)\cdot\gcd(a,b) = ab \), the least common multiple is \( \dfrac{[[a]]\cdot[[b]]}{\gcd([[a]],[[b]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the greatest common divisor, not the least common multiple.
This is the product of the two numbers, forgetting to divide by their gcd.
This divides by the gcd twice instead of once, undercorrecting.
This adds the two numbers instead of using the lcm-gcd product relationship.
This is one more than the correct lcm.
This is one less than the correct lcm.

stem: Let \( g = \gcd([[a]], [[b]]) \). What is the smallest non-negative integer \( x \) such that \( [[a]]x \equiv g \pmod{[[b]]} \)? (This \( x \) is a Bézout coefficient for \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \).)
solution: Dividing through by \( g \), solve \( \frac{a}{g}x \equiv 1 \pmod{\frac{b}{g}} \); the smallest such \( x \) is the modular inverse of \( a/g \) modulo \( b/g \), which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the gcd itself, not the Bézout coefficient \( x \).
This uses the wrong residue by subtracting from the modulus instead of taking the true smallest inverse.
This swaps which reduced value (\(a/g\) vs \(b/g\)) is inverted modulo the other.
This reports the reduced coefficient \( a/g \) itself, not its modular inverse.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

solution: Squaring both numbers squares their gcd as well: \( \gcd(a^2,b^2) = \gcd(a,b)^2 = [[answer]] \) (computed directly from \( [[a]]^2 \) and \( [[b]]^2 \)).
distractor_rationales: This uses the un-squared gcd, forgetting that squaring the numbers squares the gcd too.
This doubles the un-squared gcd instead of squaring it.
This reports the product of the squares, not their gcd.
This adds an unrelated leftover term to the un-squared gcd.
This is one more than the correct gcd.
This is one less than the correct gcd.
